blue-boar: Everything feels solid and real right now, but so does a dream, right up until the moment you wake.
cosmic-yawn: Spin this small stone one way and it spins on happily; spin it the other and it stops and reverses itself.
red-rustler: Interview hires one by one with no going back; view the first 37%, then grab the next best, to win most often.
pick-boss: A person who knows no Chinese follows a rulebook to answer notes perfectly; do they understand, or just seem to?
ghost-chef: To collect all ten cereal-box prizes you'll buy about thirty boxes; the last few hide the longest.
beast-hug: Drop a feather and a hammer where there's no air and they strike the ground at the very same instant.
meltdown: What if gems are 'grue', green until New Year then blue? Nothing you've seen so far could rule it out.
bone-pop: Flip a lamp on, off, on, twice as fast each time; after one minute, is it glowing or dark?
bone-bard: Draw a gold coin from a random box, and the chance the other coin is gold isn't half but two in three.
mad-grin: Does the word 'long' describe itself? Now ask that of 'not-self-describing' and watch the answer break.
caped-hound: A snowflake curve you could draw inside a coin, yet whose crinkled edge is infinitely long.
sly-chef: Cut a solid ball into a few strange pieces, rotate them, and rebuild two balls each the size of the first.
big-grin: A single self-referring sentence can seem to prove any wild claim you like, just by talking about itself.
chef-fang: A 100-pound sack of potatoes is 99% water; let it dry to 98% water and it now weighs just 50 pounds.
blade-prince: Tiny, mild preferences in each person can snowball a whole crowd into sharp patterns nobody actually wanted.
streetwise: Mix two jars of the same gas and nothing really changes, but mix two different gases and disorder jumps, why?
drip-dog: A perfect definition tells you precisely what a word means, yet if it's exactly right it teaches you nothing new.
neon-sage: Take one grain from a heap of sand and it's still a heap, so when exactly does it stop being one?
fond-beast: A crocodile promises to return a child if you guess its plan, so guess it won't, and watch it freeze up.
rainbow-maw: Gather just 23 people and it's already more likely than not that two of them share a birthday.
gold-sage: Add a shiny new road to a busy city and, astonishingly, everyone's drive home can get slower.
cracked-heart: A hotel with infinitely many full rooms can still fit one more guest: just ask everyone to shuffle down a door.
solar-jester: You can't comb a hairy ball perfectly flat; there's always a cowlick, and on Earth, a calm spot of wind.
shadow-pair: Pay people to catch cobras and they'll breed cobras for the reward, an incentive that creates what it fights.
blue-bones: 'The smallest number not nameable in under twelve words', which you just named using only eleven.
red-wraith: Under the right conditions, hot water can freeze into solid ice faster than cold water does.
twin-hounds: An endless trumpet shape holds only a cupful of volume, yet its surface stretches on without any limit.
alley-trio: Zigzag steps shrink toward a straight diagonal, yet their total length stays stubbornly longer than the line.
fuzz-trio: An author believes every sentence in the book, yet admits somewhere a mistake surely hides, both reasonable.
fuzz-totem: Some infinities beat others: there are more decimals between 0 and 1 than there are counting numbers.
blue-beak: If stars fill an endless universe, every line of sight should hit one, so why isn't the whole night ablaze?
feather-mage: People work differently the very moment they know they're being watched, so watching changes the result.
blue-phoenix: 'Yields falsehood when preceded by its quotation' yields falsehood when preceded by its quotation.
joy-blast: Stack forever-shrinking layers into a cake of finite volume but infinite frosting to spread.
pajama-bear: The very strengths that lift a company to the top are often what carry it too far and bring it back down.
moon-rat: It's reasonable to believe any single ticket will lose, yet unreasonable to believe every ticket will.
noodle-arms: People keep getting promoted until they land in a job they're finally bad at, and there they stay.
guffaw: A whole group takes a trip nobody wanted, each going along to please the others, who felt the exact same.
blue-gulp: In nature you must keep evolving as fast as you can just to hold your ground against everything else evolving.
masked-hare: Rocket to the stars and back and you return younger than the twin who stayed home waiting for you.
red-hound: You could fill that same horn with a finite splash of paint, yet no amount of paint could ever coat its inside.
barn-rooster: Bore a hole of equal height through a marble or a planet and the leftover rings share the same volume.
sly-imp: Tip a chain out of a jar and it leaps up into an arch above the rim before falling, pouring upward first.
ink-cat: Stir your coffee all you like and, once it settles, at least one drop ends up right where it started.
neon-court: A predictor who's never wrong sets the boxes; take one or both, and two flawless logics disagree forever.
count-fuzz: Water keeps us alive and diamonds do almost nothing, yet the useless gem costs a small fortune, why?
moon-cat: Add ten balls, remove one, faster and faster forever, and whether any remain depends on which you remove.
purple-fang: People flip their choice between the same two gambles based only on how the odds happen to be dressed up.
three-minds: One envelope holds twice the other; whichever you pick, switching seems to promise more, and never stops.
drool-ghost: One bent, one-dimensional thread can be made to pass through every point of a solid square.
coin-baron: 'All ravens are black' is quietly supported by every green apple, since each is a non-black non-raven.
blaze-grin: People happily bet on known odds over unknown ones, even when the mystery jar might pay far better.
smoke-imp: Step into a river twice and it's fresh water each time, so can you ever enter the same river again?
spun-grin: An endless list of lines, each saying 'every line below is false', yet not one can be true or false.
green-drifter: In a clever setup a particle and its own spin can travel apart, like a grin drifting off without the cat.
eruption: Swap every plank of a ship over the years and it sails on, same ship, or a new one wearing its old name?
mirror-twins: Sealed in a box with a hidden switch, a cat is somehow both asleep and awake, until the instant you peek.
neon-crown: How do you search for something new, when you wouldn't recognize the answer until you'd already found it?
glow-pair: Shake a can of mixed nuts and the biggest ones climb to the top, defying the downward pull of gravity.
last-light: A sprinkler spins one way pushing water out, but if it instead sucks water in, which way does it turn?
melt-waltz: On average, your friends have more friends than you do, and it's true for almost everyone at once.
filament: Erase the middle third again and again, and infinitely many points survive while taking up no length.
neon-garden: 'It's raining, but I don't believe it is': each half can be true, yet no one can sincerely say the whole.
riddle-cat: A barber shaves everyone who doesn't shave himself, so who on earth gets to shave the barber?
unfinished: A tiny gatekeeper sorting fast and slow molecules seems to make heat flow the wrong way, for free.
card-sharp: Among the people you meet, two unrelated traits can look linked purely because of who ended up in the room.
scarlet-mage: Wipe out the record of which path a particle took, even afterward, and its wave pattern quietly returns.
sugar-rooster: If Pinocchio says 'my nose grows now', does it? If it grows he told the truth; if not, he lied and it should.
soaked-grin: When everyone saves at once, spending dries up, and the whole economy can end up poorer for it.
kindled-rat: A ladder too long for a barn fits inside once it races fast enough, as motion squeezes its very length.
ruby-idol: No secret pre-written script can explain entangled particles; reality is stranger than any hidden plan.
mad-king: Try hard to hide something and you summon a crowd; the hushing is exactly what makes everyone look.
third-eye: Give a tortoise a head start and quick Achilles must first reach where it was, so how does he ever pass?
portal-sage: Billions of stars, billions of years, endless chances for company, so where in the cosmos is everybody?
lunar-fowl: The universe may quietly forbid every time-loop, as if nature itself stands guard over the past.
fuzz-tower: Make engines more efficient and we don't burn less fuel; we use so much more that total use climbs.
pink-nomad: Learn a new word today and suddenly it's everywhere; the world didn't change, only your attention did.
cosmic-shrug: A plain sugar pill with no medicine can genuinely ease real pain, simply because you believe that it will.
fractal-grin: Place a donkey exactly between two identical meals, equally tempting, and pure logic gives it no way to choose.
coin-alchemist: To cross a room you first cross half, then half of that, an endless list of steps before you even begin.
reach-wolf: 'This sentence is false.' If it's true then it's false, and if it's false then it's true, round and round.
tux-brain: A film can grip you with suspense on the tenth rewatch, even though you already know how it all ends.
sourpuss: The less someone knows about a skill, the more likely they are to wildly overrate how good they are at it.
shade-rise: Alternate between two games you're each guaranteed to lose, and you can somehow come out ahead.
bog-beast: Keep drilling holes in a cube forever and you're left with infinite surface but no volume at all.
sonder: Could a power great enough to lift anything at all create a stone that even it could not lift?
citrus-haze: The most perfect map of a land would have to be the land itself, so no map can ever be truly complete.
rug-cowboy: If lightning made a perfect copy of you, atom for atom, would it be you, or a brand-new stranger with your past?
white-robe: Chase happiness head-on and it slips away; it tends to arrive as a side effect of chasing something else.
neon-motel: Two partners each do better by turning on the other, yet if both stay loyal, both come out further ahead.
faded-frog: Show people solid proof they're wrong and, sometimes, they end up clinging to the mistaken belief even harder.
pink-tango: Spin a rounded little top and it flips itself upside down, rising to balance improbably on its own stem.
bow-frog: Woken once or twice by a coin you never see, how sure should you be it landed heads: one-half or one-third?
pink-tabby: You're probably nowhere special in time or space, a humbling clue to how vast the cosmos runs beyond you.
tux-frog: 'One of my two kids is a boy': the chance the other is a girl isn't half but a surprising two in three.
green-geezer: Wearing hats they can't see, a line of thinkers can guess their own colors almost perfectly using only parity.
elder-ace: Billions of years ago the sun shone far dimmer, yet early Earth stayed warm enough for liquid oceans, why?
red-tropic: Old physics said a warm object should pour out infinite blue light; fixing that riddle gave birth to quantum theory.
vapor-wave: A line so jagged it's unbroken everywhere yet has a sharp corner at every single point along it.
red-bloom: Toss a book into a black hole and its information seems erased, yet deep law says nothing is ever truly lost.
desert-robe: Waiting near the top of a tower, the first elevator to arrive is almost always heading down, and vice versa.
bat-country: Work quietly swells to fill exactly as much time as you set aside for it, however little was really needed.
checker-frog: A scientist who knows every fact about color but has only seen gray learns something new the day she sees red.
red-hood: The first chicken hatched from an egg, but that egg was laid by a chicken, so which could possibly come first?
paisley: The wisest person may simply be the one who best knows just how much they still don't know.
scrapbook: If one falling seed makes no sound, how does a whole bushel poured out make a noise? Yet it plainly does.
ink-sketch: Two ships accelerate side by side, yet the delicate string between them snaps, stretched by motion itself.
stray-loteria: In an endless cosmos, stray atoms might now and then assemble a full mind, dreaming up an entire world.
purple-haze: Give a paper loop a single half-twist and it becomes a surface with only one side and one edge.
pastel-ten: How you choose to measure light now seems to decide whether it acted as wave or particle moments ago.
fedora: Move the sickest patient from one ward to another and the average health of both wards can rise at once.
red-eyes: Nudge a few cosmic constants a hair and stars, planets, and life vanish; ours sits improbably just right.
gold-chain: At every moment two spots on exact opposite sides of Earth share the same temperature and air pressure.
silver-fox: Cut a pizza in eight through any off-center point, and two friends taking alternate slices get equal shares.
glitch: Share a pasture and each herder gains by adding one more animal, until the grass everyone needs is gone.
free: An arrow must bend around the very bow it's shot from, wobbling in flight yet flying straight to the mark.
the-crowd: A tiny ant crawls along a rubber band that stretches longer every second, yet somehow still reaches the end.
velvet-lounge: Ask for a 'random' chord of a circle and the odds it's long depend entirely on what 'random' even means.
reefer: A sailboat can slice along faster than the very wind that's pushing it, and even sail against that wind.
bow-cat: Measure a coast with a shorter ruler and it grows longer; keep zooming in and its length has no end.
spawn: Watch a quantum system closely enough and your constant looking can freeze it; the watched pot never boils.
cloud-cat: A robot face grows more charming as it nears human, then, just short of real, it turns oddly unsettling.
rainbow-elder: Nations sitting on oil or gold often grow poorer and less stable than neighbors with far less to dig up.
old-friends: You're paid a fortune just to intend to sip a harmless toxin tomorrow, but can you intend what you'll never do?
swole-toad: Add 1 minus 1 plus 1 minus 1 forever: group it one way you get zero, another way you get one.
kinchui: Two allies must attack together, but no messenger can ever confirm that the last message got through.
small-counsel: The longer a book, idea, or tool has already survived, the longer you can expect it to keep going.
candidate: In any rich system of math there are true statements it can never prove using its own rules.
purple-scarf: Picture the set of all sets that don't contain themselves; does it contain itself? Either answer breaks.
greenback: The more conditions improve, the more the injustices that remain start to feel unbearable rather than smaller.
plaid-elder: The sharper you pin down where a particle is, the blurrier how fast it moves becomes, by nature's design.
pipe-dream: A machine could feed you a lifetime of perfect joy, so why does something in us still want the real thing?
lotus-elder: If each of us had a private box we call 'beetle', the word could work fine even if no two boxes matched.
rain-rider: A machine scans you here and builds a perfect copy there, so does you step out on the far side, or a twin?
free-throw: Roll one coin once around another of the same size and it turns twice, not once, before it's home again.
red-fez: The tortoise makes Achilles justify each logic step, then justify that step, so can reasoning ever get moving?
pastel-duo: A water drop on a scorching pan skates around for ages, gliding on a thin cushion of its own steam.
gold-crooner: A staircase that climbs steadily from bottom to top while staying flat almost everywhere it goes.
turquoise: What happens when an unstoppable force meets an immovable object? If both truly exist, the words undo themselves.
space-pink: Rearrange the same four puzzle pieces and an empty hole appears from nowhere, so where did the area go?
mug-shots: Your wait for the bus feels long because you're more likely to arrive during a longer-than-average gap.
glitter-grin: Some luxuries actually sell better the pricier they get; raise the tag and, oddly, more people want to own it.
red-guard: Every number is interesting, because the very first boring one would be fascinating for exactly that reason.
laughing-elder: Opposite edges of the sky share the same temperature, though they seem too far apart to have ever met.
green-velvet: In a coin-flip race between two three-flip patterns, whoever picks second can always tilt the odds their way.
nap-stack: A bottle whose inside is its outside, a vessel that holds nothing because it has no real insides.
pink-room: There are exactly as many perfect squares as whole numbers, even though most numbers aren't squares.
number-nine: A big wheel and a small one fixed together roll the same distance, so do their two circles somehow match?
cloud-collage: A traveler carries a song to the past, where it's copied and handed forward to be carried back; who wrote it?
mad-goggles: Test positive for a rare disease with a great test, and you're still probably fine; the rarity outweighs it.
red-cackle: We weep for characters we know were never real, so how can wholly made-up people move us so genuinely?
orange-wrap: Offer someone twenty jams instead of three and they're less likely to buy, and less happy if they do.
long-carry: Send particles one at a time through two slits and they slowly paint a wave, as if each went through both.
number-five: Chill pure water below freezing and it stays perfectly liquid, until one tap turns it all to ice in a blink.
rainbow-gate: Expect more from someone and they often rise to meet it, belief quietly reshaping what they can actually do.
pure-joy: Three rings can lock together so cleverly that removing any one frees the other two completely.
blue-idol: A teacher promises a surprise quiz next week; clever students prove it's impossible, then it surprises them.
parlor-scene: A hollow sphere can be turned inside out through itself, smoothly, without a single crease or tear.
banquet: No program can ever exist that reliably tells whether every other program will finish or loop forever.
chrome-blank: Three dice can be built so the first usually beats the second, the second the third, and the third the first.
bronze-think: We know how to ride a bike or recognize a face, yet can't fully spell out the rules we're actually following.
laser-stare: You can't learn what you already know, nor what you don't, so how does anyone learn anything at all?
royal-purple: If every truth could in principle be known, then strangely it follows that every truth is already known.
orange-monk: Add 1 plus 2 plus 3 forever and a strange, deep kind of math hands you back negative one-twelfth.
night-elder: For computers, hard chess is easy while a toddler's easy tasks, seeing and walking, are staggeringly hard.
citrus-pool: Pluck one hair and no one turns bald; keep going and a full head becomes bare, but at which single hair?
madiba: A coin game with an infinite average payout, so why would almost no one pay much to sit down and play?
cactus-walk: One straight cut can perfectly halve the bread, the cheese, and the filling all at once, however they lie.
white-tux: Early calculus treated tiny amounts as both something and nothing, 'ghosts of departed quantities' that worked.
red-sparkle: Measure one of two linked particles and its partner answers instantly, even a galaxy away, spookily so.
the-touch: A hundred players each open half the lockers to find their name, yet one looping trick lets nearly all win.
black-tie: If a future civilization could run vast, perfect world-simulations, how sure are you that you aren't in one?
crimson-robe: In heaps of real-world numbers, far more begin with a 1 than a 9, a hidden bias baked into the digits.
low-rider: Bet again and again against a far richer player on a fair coin, and given time you'll almost surely run out.
peace-sign: Pick one of three doors, then switch after a losing one opens, and your odds of winning quietly double.
big-laugh: A teacher's fee is due once the student wins a case, so their very first case is over that fee, and loops.
rogue-sheet: For a few staple foods, raising the price can make people buy even more of them rather than less.
pink-prophet: The better our machines run themselves, the rustier our own skills grow for the rare moment they fail.
soft-ghost: Physics runs the same forwards or backwards, yet cups shatter and never unshatter, so where is 'forward'?
blacklight-kid: At every frozen instant a flying arrow sits perfectly still, so where does its motion actually hide?
white-garden: Stir your tea and the leaves gather in the middle, drifting against the swirl instead of flinging outward.
the-scholar: A trend can hold in every single group, yet flip completely the moment you pool those groups together.
green-fuzz: Sneak in a division by a cleverly hidden zero and you can 'prove' that 1 equals 2, math's favorite magic trick.
disco-wire: Math proves some infinities are uncountably vast, yet its own rules fit inside a tiny countable world.
drip-claus: Somewhere in a big city two strangers have the exact same number of hairs on their heads, guaranteed.
silver-press: Once basic needs are met, a richer country isn't reliably a happier one over the long run.
number-six: The number 0.999... with nines running on forever isn't merely close to 1, it is exactly and provably 1.
pixel-madiba: One vote almost never decides an election, so by cold logic why bother? And yet millions happily do.
white-turban: One card says the back is true; the back says the front is false, each side quietly undoing the other.
court-cackle: Computers landed on every desk, yet for years the promised boom barely showed up in the numbers.
holo-blank: To study how people truly talk you must watch them, but being watched changes how they talk.
two-legends: A thrown dart is sure to land on the board, yet the chance of hitting any one exact point is precisely zero.
prism-prayer: No voting system can turn everyone's honest rankings into a group choice that obeys every fair rule at once.
silver-stroll: A group can prefer A to B, B to C, and C to A, so the majority's will loops with no clear winner.
gold-kid: In a perfect, frictionless fluid a moving object would feel no drag at all, so how does anything slow down?
motllociater: Spin a disk fast enough and its rim shrinks while its width stays put, bending the circle's own geometry.
